summary: Tractate Yoma daf 86b covers: R. Yochanan: teshuva is so great it overrides a divine decree; R. Yitzchak: cancel your evil decree before the New Year; What circumstances constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scholar?; R. Yosei bar Yehuda: baalei teshuva and their elevated standing; Tosefta on transgressing against teachers and communal transgressions.

display.whats: R. Yochanan: teshuva is so great it overrides a divine decree
display.hint: R. Yochanan declares that teshuva is so powerful it can override even a divine decree of punishment - repentance changes the outcome of divine judgment.
display.title: Teshuva Overrides a Divine Decree

learning.ahaMoment: If God's decree responds to your current state, and your current state genuinely changes through teshuva, then the decree can change. Divine justice is not a machine - it is a relationship.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Yochanan: teshuva is so great it overrides a divine decree. Genuine change in you produces genuine change in God's response.
learning.learnerQuestion: Can repentance actually change a divine decree of punishment that has already been pronounced?
learning.coreTension: Between the apparent finality of a divine decree (God has decided) and the possibility that human repentance can alter what God has already determined.
learning.coreMove: R. Yochanan declares that teshuva is so powerful it can override a divine decree. This is not a philosophical contradiction but a theological claim about the nature of divine judgment: God's decrees respond to human reality, and genuine change in the human produces genuine change in the divine response.

takeaway.text: R. Yochanan's teaching that teshuva overrides divine decrees reflects the Talmudic theology that divine judgment is relational and responsive - it tracks the human's actual moral state rather than operating as an independent mechanism.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may see divine decrees as unchangeable; R. Yochanan's teaching requires understanding divine justice as relational rather than mechanical.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yochanan: repentance overrides a Torah prohibition
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yochanan: great is repentance, for it overrides even a Torah prohibition. A divorced, remarried woman may not return to her first husband (Deuteronomy 24:4); yet 'would you return to Me?' (Jeremiah 3:1) invites Israel, the metaphorical wayward wife, back to God. Repentance overrides the analogous bar.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yonatan: repentance hastens redemption
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yonatan
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yonatan: great is repentance, for it brings the redemption closer - 'a redeemer will come to Zion, and to those who repent from transgression in Jacob' (Isaiah 59:20). The redeemer comes BECAUSE there are penitents in Jacob.

argumentFlow.label: Reish Lakish: sins become errors, or merits
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: Reish Lakish: repentance turns intentional sins into unwitting ones - 'you have stumbled in your iniquity' (Hosea 14:2) calls deliberate sin mere stumbling. But he also said repentance turns sins into actual merits ('the wicked who turns... shall live by them'). Resolution: repentance from love turns sins into merits; repentance from fear turns them into unwitting errors.

argumentFlow.label: R. Shmuel bar Nachmani: repentance lengthens life
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Shmuel bar Nachmani
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachmani in Rabbi Yonatan's name: great is repentance, for it lengthens a person's life - 'when the wicked turns from his wickedness... he shall preserve his life' (Ezekiel 18:27).

theological: Teshuva overrides divine decree: repentance is not just a legal mechanism but a
narrative: R. Yochanan: teshuva is so great it overrides a divine decre

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of R. Yochanan context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Repentance always converts a person's sins into merits.
misconceptions.correction: Only repentance from love turns sins into merits. Repentance from fear downgrades intentional sins to unwitting errors but does not convert them into merits.

visualizableElements.name: R. Yochanan: teshuva is so great it overrides a di

quizSeeds.question: How does Reish Lakish reconcile his two statements that repentance turns sins into unwitting errors and into merits?
quizSeeds.answer: Repentance from love transforms intentional sins into merits; repentance from fear transforms them into unwitting errors. The motivation determines how fully the past is reframed.

quizSeeds.question: What prohibition does Rabbi Yochanan say repentance overrides, and from which verse?
quizSeeds.answer: The bar on a remarried divorcee returning to her first husband (Deuteronomy 24:4). Yet Jeremiah 3:1 invites Israel, the metaphorical wayward wife, back to God, showing repentance overrides the analogous prohibition.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Yochanan declares that teshuva is so powerful it can override even a divine decree of punishment - repentance changes the outcome of divine judgmen

display.whats: R. Yitzchak: cancel your evil decree before the New Year
display.hint: R. Yitzchak advises: cancel your evil decree (through teshuva and good deeds) before the New Year arrives - the window of judgment can be addressed proactively.
display.title: Cancel Your Evil Decree Before the New Year

learning.ahaMoment: The window before the New Year is itself a window of opportunity. R. Yitzchak is teaching that proactive repentance - before the decree is issued - is the most powerful form.
learning.memoryAnchor: Cancel your decree before the New Year: proactive teshuva before Rosh Hashanah is more powerful than reactive teshuva after.
learning.learnerQuestion: What does R. Yitzchak mean by advising people to 'cancel their evil decree' before the New Year?
learning.coreTension: Between the idea that the High Holy Day period (Rosh Hashanah through YK) is the time for repentance and R. Yitzchak's urgency about acting before the New Year arrives.
learning.coreMove: R. Yitzchak teaches that the window before Rosh Hashanah is also a time to repent and cancel a decree of punishment. Waiting for the formal season may be too late to undo what is being decided; proactive repentance before the decree is issued is more effective than reactive repentance after.

takeaway.text: R. Yitzchak's advice frames repentance as proactive rather than reactive: cancel your decree by repenting before Rosh Hashanah, not just during the ten days of repentance after.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think repentance is primarily a response to the High Holy Day season; R. Yitzchak reframes it as something that should anticipate the season.

argumentFlow.label: God is unlike a human you have wronged
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yitzchak
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yitzchak, citing the West in the name of Rabba bar Mari: a human you insult may or may not be appeased, and even if appeased, perhaps not by words alone - you may need much more. God's measure is different.

argumentFlow.label: God is appeased by words alone
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: For a private transgression against God, He is appeased by mere words - 'take with you words and return to God' (Hosea 14:3). More: He treats it as a favor done to Him ('accept that which is good'), credits it as if one offered bulls ('the offering of our lips'), and lest you think only obligatory bulls, 'I will love them freely' - it is reckoned as a freewill offering.

argumentFlow.label: One penitent can save the world
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Meir
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Meir: great is repentance, for the whole world is forgiven on account of one individual who repents - 'I will love them freely, for My anger has turned away from him' (Hosea 14:5). It says 'from him,' not 'from them': because that one repented, everyone is healed.

theological: Cancel your evil decree proactively: the Rosh Hashana period is the moment when
narrative: R. Yitzchak: cancel your evil decree before the New Year

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of R. Yitzchak context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Appeasing God for a private sin requires the same effort as appeasing an offended person.
misconceptions.correction: Unlike a human, who may refuse or demand more than words, God is appeased for a private sin by words alone, and even credits the repentance as a freewill offering.

visualizableElements.name: R. Yitzchak: cancel your evil decree before the Ne

quizSeeds.question: What is the central contrast Rabbi Yitzchak draws between God and a human you have wronged?
quizSeeds.answer: A wronged human may refuse to be appeased, or may demand more than words. God, for a private sin against Him, is appeased by words alone and even treats the repentance as a favor and a freewill offering.

quizSeeds.question: What does Rabbi Meir derive from 'My anger has turned away from him' (singular)?
quizSeeds.answer: That the entire world can be forgiven because of one individual who repents. The verse says 'from him,' not 'from them,' teaching that one person's repentance brings healing to all.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Yitzchak advises: cancel your evil decree (through teshuva and good deeds) before the New Year arrives - the window of judgment can be addressed pr

display.whats: What circumstances constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scholar?
display.hint: The Gemara specifies what actions constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scholar: behaviors that appear improper even when technically permitted.
display.title: Chilul Hashem for Scholars: Specific Cases

learning.ahaMoment: The examples are a mirror: every scholar must ask not just 'is this permitted?' but 'what does this say to an observer about what Torah produces?' That second question is the chilul Hashem lens.
learning.memoryAnchor: Scholar's chilul Hashem examples: buying on credit, haughty walk - each teaches the pattern: how does this look to an observer?
learning.learnerQuestion: What specific behaviors constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scholar?
learning.coreTension: Between the general principle (behavior that creates bad impressions is chilul Hashem for scholars) and the need for concrete examples that practitioners can identify and avoid.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara gives concrete examples: buying meat on credit (appearing to defer payment dishonestly), walking with a haughty gait, handling money carelessly in public. Each example shows how a scholar's visible behavior creates impressions about what Torah-educated people are like.

takeaway.text: The Gemara's specific examples of scholarly chilul Hashem show that the test is always: 'does this behavior create a negative impression of Torah and God in the observer?' The examples teach the pattern, not an exhaustive list.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may memorize the examples without internalizing the pattern; the examples are illustrations of a principle, not a closed checklist.

argumentFlow.label: What proves repentance is complete?
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: What demonstrates complete repentance? Rav Yehuda: when the same temptation comes to a person's hand a first and second time and he resists. He illustrated: the same woman, same time, same place as the original sin, everything aligned - and this time he overcomes his urge. That proves the repentance is real.

argumentFlow.label: When to publicize a sin
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav / Rav Nachman
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yehuda cited a contradiction: 'fortunate is he whose sin is hidden' (Psalms 32:1) versus 'he who conceals his transgressions shall not prosper' (Proverbs 28:13). Resolution: a publicized sin should have its repentance publicized too; a private sin should not be publicized. Rav Zutra (Rav Nachman): sins against another person should be made public so people can press the victim to forgive; sins against God need not be publicized.

theological: Scholar's chilul Hashem: behavior that appears questionable from the outside can
narrative: What circumstances constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scho

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: All sins should be confessed publicly, or all kept private.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara distinguishes: a public sin or one against another person warrants public repentance, while a private sin against God should be kept hidden.

visualizableElements.name: What circumstances constitute chilul Hashem for a

quizSeeds.question: By Rav Yehuda's standard, what proves a person's repentance is complete?
quizSeeds.answer: Facing the very same temptation - same person, same time, same place as the original sin - and resisting it. Overcoming the identical situation proves the repentance is genuine.

quizSeeds.question: When should one publicize a sin, and when keep it private?
quizSeeds.answer: A sin already public, or one committed against another person, should have its repentance publicized (so people can urge the victim to forgive). A private sin against God should not be publicized, per 'fortunate is he whose sin is hidden.'

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara specifies what actions constitute chilul Hashem for a Torah scholar: behaviors that appear improper even when technically permitted.

display.whats: R. Yosei bar Yehuda: baalei teshuva and their elevated standing
display.hint: R. Yosei bar Yehuda teaches that baalei teshuva (penitents) have an especially elevated standing - in some ways they stand higher than the completely righteous.
display.title: Baalei Teshuva: Elevated Beyond the Righteous

learning.ahaMoment: The elevation comes from the return, not the sin. The baal teshuva knows from experience what it cost them to return, and that knowledge is a form of intimacy with God unavailable to those who never had to fight their way back.
learning.memoryAnchor: Baal teshuva stands where the perfectly righteous cannot reach: the depth of return exceeds the depth of constancy.
learning.learnerQuestion: In what sense do penitents (baalei teshuva) have higher standing than those who were never sinful?
learning.coreTension: Between the intuition that those who never sinned are 'more righteous' and R. Yosei bar Yehuda's teaching that baalei teshuva actually stand higher in some respect.
learning.coreMove: R. Yosei bar Yehuda teaches that baalei teshuva reach a place that even the entirely righteous cannot attain, because the depth of the return process - the shattering and rebuilding - produces a relationship to God that the never-tested cannot access. The difficulty of return is itself the elevation.

takeaway.text: Baalei teshuva reach a level inaccessible to those who never sinned because the depth of the return process - having fallen and chosen to rise - creates a quality of relationship with God that comes only through the experience of genuine return.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may find this counterintuitive - why should sinners have an advantage? The key is that it is not the sin but the return that produces the elevation.

argumentFlow.label: Forgiven three times, not the fourth
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yosei bar Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yosei bar Yehuda: a person who transgresses is forgiven a first, second, and third time, but not the fourth - 'for three transgressions of Israel, but for four I will not reverse it' (Amos 2:6). Repeated, unrepented sin eventually exhausts forgiveness.

argumentFlow.label: Why the second proof-verse?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara asks why a second verse ('all these things God does twice or three times with a man,' Job 33:29) was needed. Answer: lest you think God's patience for three offenses applies only to a community and not to an individual, the Job verse extends it to the individual too. After that, forgiveness ceases.

theological: Baalei teshuva above the perfectly righteous: the one who has returned from sin
narrative: R. Yosei bar Yehuda: baalei teshuva and their elevated stand

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of baalei teshuva context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: God's threefold patience applies only to the nation as a whole.
misconceptions.correction: The Job verse ('twice or three times with a man') extends the same threefold patience to an individual, after which forgiveness for the unrepented sin ceases.

visualizableElements.name: R. Yosei bar Yehuda: baalei teshuva and their elev

quizSeeds.question: How many times is a repeat transgressor forgiven according to Rabbi Yosei bar Yehuda, and what is the source?
quizSeeds.answer: Three times; not the fourth. From 'for three transgressions of Israel, but for four I will not reverse it' (Amos 2:6).

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Gemara cite the second verse from Job (33:29)?
quizSeeds.answer: To extend the three-chances principle from a community to an individual. Without it, one might think only a community gets three forgivenesses; Job's 'with a man' shows the same applies to an individual.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Yosei bar Yehuda teaches that baalei teshuva (penitents) have an especially elevated standing - in some ways they stand higher than the completely

display.whats: Tosefta on transgressing against teachers and communal transgressions
display.hint: The Tosefta discusses the severity of transgressing against one's teacher and communal forms of chilul Hashem.
display.title: Tosefta: Transgressions Against Teachers and Communal Chilul Hashem

learning.ahaMoment: The who matters in repair, not just the what. Harming a teacher is harming an institution; harming the community's standing is harming a collective. The repair must match the level of the harm.
learning.memoryAnchor: Tosefta: teacher-transgression and communal chilul Hashem both require repair at their own scale - individual apology is insufficient.
learning.learnerQuestion: What does the Tosefta teach about transgressions against teachers and communal forms of chilul Hashem?
learning.coreTension: Between sins between individuals (which can be addressed through direct appeasement) and transgressions against authority figures (teachers) or the community, which may require different repair.
learning.coreMove: The Tosefta teaches that transgressions against a teacher carry the heightened weight of damaging a relationship that represents Torah itself, and that communal chilul Hashem - disrespecting the community's moral standing - requires communal repair, not just personal atonement.

takeaway.text: Transgressions against teachers and communal forms of chilul Hashem require repair at the level at which the damage occurred; damaging a teacher-Torah relationship or a community's moral standing cannot be repaired solely through individual confession.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may treat all interpersonal sins as equivalent; the Tosefta highlights that the role of the person harmed (teacher, community) shapes the repair process.

argumentFlow.label: Do not re-confess a forgiven sin
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: The Sages taught: a sin confessed one Yom Kippur is not re-confessed the next, since it is already forgiven. But if he repeated the sin during the year, he confesses it again. And if he did not repeat it yet confesses it anyway, of him it says 'as a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly' (Proverbs 26:11).

argumentFlow.label: R. Eliezer ben Yaakov: re-confessing is praiseworthy
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Eliezer ben Yaakov
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: one who re-confesses earlier sins is all the more praiseworthy, recalling them with humility - 'my sin is ever before me' (Psalms 51:5). So how does he read 'a dog returns to its vomit'? Per Rav Huna: once a person repeats a sin it becomes 'as if permitted' to him - that is the fool's folly, not the act of re-confessing.

argumentFlow.label: Detail the sin, or not?
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yehuda ben Bava / R. Akiva
argumentFlow.text: Must one specify the sin in confession? Rabbi Yehuda ben Bava: yes - Moses said 'they made themselves a god of gold' (Exodus 32:31), detailing it. Rabbi Akiva: no - 'fortunate is he whose sin is hidden' (Psalms 32:1). And Moses' words, per Rabbi Yannai, were really a defense: it was the abundance of gold You gave Israel that led to the calf.

argumentFlow.label: Moses vs. David on recording disgrace
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Two great leaders: Moses asked that his sin be written explicitly ('because you did not believe in Me,' Numbers 20:12); David asked that his not be written ('fortunate is he whose sin is hidden').

argumentFlow.label: The parable of the two flogged women
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A parable: two women flogged in court, one for forbidden relations, one for eating Sabbatical-year unripe figs. The fig-eater asked the court to publicize her lighter offense so people would not assume she sinned as gravely as the other. So Moses had his sin publicized so people would not think he committed the graver sins of his generation - the calf or the spies.

argumentFlow.label: Exposing hypocrites
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: One exposes hypocrites because of the chilul Hashem - so people do not mistake them for the righteous and imitate them, 'when a righteous man turns and commits iniquity, I will lay a stumbling block before him' (Ezekiel 3:20). A hidden wicked person leads others astray; revealing him prevents that desecration.

argumentFlow.label: The danger of authority and ease
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The tranquility of the wicked leads to their ruin, as in their ease they dwell on forbidden things. Authority buries the one who holds it: naked he entered power and naked he leaves - if only his exit were as clean as his entrance, without added sin.

argumentFlow.label: Rav and Rava's words before judging
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav / Rava
argumentFlow.text: When Rav went to judge he would say of himself: of his own will he goes to die (a judge who errs is liable to Heaven), he does not do his household's will, and he returns empty-handed - if only his entrance were like his exit, free of sin. Rava, going to judge, would say his own version (continued on 87a).

theological: Transgressing against teachers: some communal and relational sins have their own
narrative: Tosefta on transgressing against teachers and communal trans

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Re-confessing a past sin is always wrong, 'like a dog returning to its vomit.'
misconceptions.correction: Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov holds re-confessing is praiseworthy humility. The 'dog' verse is reinterpreted to describe a sin one has repeated until it feels permitted - not the act of recalling a forgiven sin.

visualizableElements.name: Tosefta on transgressing against teachers and comm

quizSeeds.question: Should one re-confess on Yom Kippur a sin already confessed the previous year? Whose views differ?
quizSeeds.answer: The baraita says no - it is already forgiven, and re-confessing is 'like a dog returning to its vomit.' Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov says re-confessing is praiseworthy as an act of humility, reading the 'dog' verse instead per Rav Huna's idea that a repeated sin feels 'as if permitted.'

quizSeeds.question: What does the parable of the two flogged women teach about Moses?
quizSeeds.answer: The woman flogged for the lighter sin wanted it publicized so people would not assume she committed the graver one. Likewise Moses had his sin written explicitly so people would not think he was guilty of the calf or the spies.

quizSeeds.question: Why does one expose hypocrites, according to the sugya?
quizSeeds.answer: Because of the chilul Hashem they cause: if people mistake a secretly wicked person for righteous and imitate him, they are led astray. Exposing him prevents that desecration of God's name.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Tosefta discusses the severity of transgressing against one's teacher and communal forms of chilul Hashem.

rashiTranslations.he: לא תעשה שבתורה. לא יוכל בעלה הראשון וגו': כי כשלת בעונך.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: R. Yochanan on the power of teshuva to override divine decrees.

rashiTranslations.he: עונך יחשב כמכשול שהיא שגגה: עליהם (חיו) [הוא] יחיה. על כל מה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: R. Yitzchak advises addressing the decree before Rosh Hashana.

rashiTranslations.he: שעשה ואף על העבירות: מתפייס הימנו. מקבל הימנו פיוסים:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: actions that appear questionable from the outside are chilul Hashem for scholars.

rashiTranslations.he: ונשלמה פרים שפתינו. במקום פרים: נדבה. מקובלת ברצון יותר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: baalei teshuva stand higher than the completely righteous in some respects.

rashiTranslations.he: מחובה: ארפא משובתם. מן היחיד:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: transgressing against one's teacher has its own halachic gravity.

rashiTranslations.he: שבא דבר עבירה לידו. שנכשל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: בה כבר: באותה אשה באותו מקום.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: שעבר שם העבירה ואותו פרק
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: מתוך שדומה מכל וכל לזמן שנכשל בו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: כבר יצרו מתגבר עליו ואומר לו ראה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: פלונית ואותו מקום ואותו פרק הוא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: קום עשה מה שעשית כבר: כסוי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: חטאה. שלא נתגלה עונו: בחטא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: מפורסם. טוב לו שיודה ויתבייש ולא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 86b.

rashiTranslations.he: יכפור בו: בחטא שאינו מפורסם.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לא יגלה חטאו וכבוד השם הוא שכל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מה שאדם חוטא בפרהסיא מיעוט
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: כבוד שמים הוא: עבירות שבין אדם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לחבירו. יגלה לרבים שיבקשו ממנו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שימחול לו: וחטאתי נגדי תמיד.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: איני סבור שכפרת לי והרי הוא תמיד
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: כאילו הוא נגדי עומד: וצריך לפרט
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: את החטא. כשהוא מתודה אומר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: תטא פלוני חטאתי: כדר' ינאי.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לא הזכיר העון אלא למעטו ולומר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אתה גרמת להם משל לאדם שהיה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לו בן נאה ומאכילו ומשקהו ותולה לו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: כיס של דינרין בצוארו והושיבו בפתח
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: זונות מה יעשה שלא יחטא: הכי גרס בספרי משה אמר יכתב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: סורחני שנאמר יען לא האמנתם בי. בכל מקום שנכתב מיתתו שם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: נכתב סרחונו. אלמא ניחא למכתביה זמנין טובא: לשתי נשים.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שלוקות: אחת קלקלה. זנתה והתרו בה למלקות כך דור המדבר מתו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: במדבר שהאמינו למרגלים (משה) מת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: במדבר על שמעו נא המורים אמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: יכתב סורחני שלא יאמרו אף אני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: קלקלתי כמותם: פגי שביעית. תאנים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שלא בישלו כל צרכן ורחמנא אמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: והיתה שבת הארץ לכם לאכלה (ויקרא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כה) לאכלה ולא להפסד: מפרסמין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אח החנפין. שהן רשעים ומראין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: עצמן כצדיקים אם יש מכיר במעשיו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: מצוה לפרסמו מפני חילול השם שבני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אדם למידין ממעשיו שסבורין עליו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'people learn from his deeds thinking him righteous' (שסבורין עליו שהוא צדיק): when a hypocrite's wickedness is hidden, people imitate him believing he is righteous - that is the chilul Hashem that justifies exposing him publicly.

rashiTranslations.he: שהוא צדיק ועוד כשבא עליו פורענות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues: moreover, when punishment comes upon him, people say 'what did his righteousness accomplish for him?' - implying that righteous conduct is worthless. This compounds the desecration, because his apparent piety dragged God's name into disrepute along with his downfall.

rashiTranslations.he: בני אדם אומרים מה הועיל לו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'people say what did it profit him' (מה הועיל לו): bystanders see a seemingly righteous person suffer and conclude that piety provides no protection - a profound chilul Hashem, since observers lose faith in reward and punishment.

rashiTranslations.he: זכותו: שנאמר ובשוב צדיק מצדקו.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'as it is written, when a righteous man turns' (ובשוב צדיק מצדקו): Ezekiel 3:20 is cited - when someone who appeared righteous turns to wickedness, God places a stumbling block before him, meaning He arranges that his sins become publicly exposed.

rashiTranslations.he: סיפיה דקרא ונתתי מכשול לפניו אני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses the end of that verse 'I will place a stumbling block before him, I' (ונתתי מכשול לפניו אני): God Himself engineers the exposure, bringing about a visible sin so that the hypocrite's true character becomes known and people cease to be misled by him.

rashiTranslations.he: ממציא לידו חטא גלוי לכל שיכשל בו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'I bring to his hand an overt sin so he stumbles in it' (ממציא לידו חטא גלוי לכל שיכשל בו) - God arranges a public transgression specifically so that the man's wickedness is revealed to all, preventing further desecration caused by his false reputation.

rashiTranslations.he: וידעו את מעשיו: המוחלטין. רשעים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'the confirmed wicked' (המוחלטין): those who are definitively, thoroughly wicked - not merely occasional sinners - are the ones referred to by the Gemara's warning that their tranquility eventually becomes their downfall.

rashiTranslations.he: גמורין: שלות רשעים סופה תקלה.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'the tranquility of the wicked is their eventual ruin' (שלות רשעים סופה תקלה): because the wicked live without hardship or fear, they have leisure to dwell on sinful thoughts and act on them, which ultimately leads to their catastrophe.

rashiTranslations.he: מתוך שלותם הם יושבין ומהרהרין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'from their ease they sit and meditate on sins' (מתוך שלותם הם יושבין ומהרהרין בעבירות) - a person under pressure has no time to plan wrongdoing, but one living comfortably in ease and wealth finds his mind drifting toward forbidden things.

rashiTranslations.he: בעבירות: והרשות. הרבנות:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'and authority' (והרשות): this refers to communal leadership and rabbinic authority (הרבנות). The next line explains why accepting such a position is dangerous even for a righteous person.

rashiTranslations.he: מקברת בעליה. שנאמר (שמות א)
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'buries its holders' with a proof from Exodus 1: it is written that Joseph died first and only afterward all his brothers - implying that his early death was a consequence of the authority he held, since leadership exposes one to sins one would otherwise never encounter.

rashiTranslations.he: וימת יוסף תחילה ואח"כ כל אחיו :
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues the proof: Joseph died first, before all his brothers, despite being younger than several of them - because he took on the yoke of royal authority, which the Gemara says 'buries' those who exercise it.

rashiTranslations.he: ולואי שתהא יציאה. ממנה בלא חטא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'if only the exit from authority were without sin as the entry was' (ולואי שתהא יציאה ממנה בלא חטא כביאה): a person enters a position of power with clean hands, but the longer he holds it the more likely he accumulates sins of judgment, favoritism, or error - so Rav would pray to leave as clean as he entered.

rashiTranslations.he: כביאה: נפיק לדינא. להיות דן דין:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'he goes out to judge' (נפיק לדינא - להיות דן דין): this is the moment Rav would recite his prayer - upon leaving to sit in judgment - because a judge who errs even inadvertently bears heavenly liability, making each session an act of self-sacrifice.

rashiTranslations.he: בצבו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi's text breaks off with 'בצבו' (possibly 'willingly' or a corrupt fragment), marking the end of 86b at the page break. The continuation of Rava's prayer appears on 87a.
